behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is algorithm quality in the heuristic itself (route construction + length calculation), not I/O or cross-process behavior. No real OS/network/subprocess is required, and no AI/ML output is involved. Higher levels were rejected because the behavior text is strictly about approximation ratio for bounded inputs, which can be reproduced with in-memory coordinates and an in-process optimal solver baseline.
behaviors.test_description: Call the heuristic function on many deterministic coordinate sets (2..20 stops), compute exact optimal length in-process (e.g., Held-Karp/brute force for bounded n), and assert heuristic_length/optimal_length <= 1.5 for each case.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: This is deterministic formula/lookup logic (time = distance/speed by road type). The failure can be caught at a local function/object boundary with in-memory distances and config maps. Integration was considered, but behavior text does not require external config loading, DB, filesystem, or network; it only states computation from distance + configured speed.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the estimator with explicit segment distances and road-type speed config provided in-memory; assert per-segment and total window outputs match expected arithmetic, including mixed road types and rounding/units edge cases.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: Failure is pure validation/business-rule logic on numeric inputs (estimated time vs remaining shift). No module interaction beyond a local validator is required to surface the bug. Higher levels are disqualified because behavior text specifies deterministic comparison and excess calculation only.
behaviors.test_description: Test the validator function with route durations above, below, and equal to remaining shift hours; assert reject/accept decision and exact excess amount (including boundary and precision cases).

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: Real failure appears at module handoff boundaries: geocoding result handling -> stop filtering -> optimizer input -> skipped-address reporting. A unit test of any single function could miss orchestration defects. System was considered but rejected because the behavior text targets logical handling of a known API response shape ('returns no results'), which can be reproduced with an in-process geocoder stub; no environment-dependent HTTP behavior is required. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Wire service + geocoder adapter interface + optimizer in-process; stub geocoder so one address yields empty result and others return coordinates. Assert output route excludes failed address, optimization runs on remaining stops, and skipped-address list contains exactly the failed input.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is cross-module data flow: deduplication/aggregation plus routing input/output consistency ('not visited twice'). Unit-testing only a dedupe helper could miss mismatch between consolidated stops and optimizer visitation. System is disqualified because no real OS/network/subprocess behavior is needed; Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because no AI steps exist.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service pipeline in-process with duplicate addresses carrying distinct items; assert output has one consolidated stop for that address, items are merged correctly, and final route contains that stop exactly once.
